column5: In order of when I finished.

column2: The Killing of a Sacred Deer
column5: This shit was not fun to relive bro, even just reading the words on the page was painful... Incredibly faithful to the movie though. Only notable changes are that, in the screenplay, it takes place in UK (how they ended up filming in Cincinnati is so beyond me lmao, maybe that was the only city with a hospital willing to let them film there) and is set in 1999. I do wonder how he extended his 87 page screenplay into a 110 minute film though... only a rewatch will tell. Fascinating how Yorgos really does just write a normal domestic drama for the first half, albeit with the creepy presence of Martin trying to make Steven his dad. Barry Keoghan's casting was a stroke of genius. If you had no idea who Lanthimos was, reading this for the first time, you'd just assume it was a regular old family drama... then Bob loses feeling in his legs and shit just keeps hitting the fan from there on out. But there's still that lingering darkness from the very first page; the opening shot is an open heart surgery with all the organs visible, so you know right away that something is off. If I were reading this as a producer before the movie was made, I don't even know what I'd compare it to. Martin's character reminded me of Donnie Darko, there are some parallels to Funny Games here and there, and the long monologues all throughout give it a very stage play feel, which makes sense given that it's based on a Greek tragedy (the ethics of it all are fascinating too. A sacrifice for a sacrifice, etc.) but is just completely cinematic. Maybe also von Trier with the classical music and slo-mo shots. I suppose there's a Kubrickian quality to the visuals, but the screenplay definitely didn't remind me of Eyes Wide Shut or anything.
